pattern_recognition: Graph Traversal - The problem involves exploring a matrix as a graph where each cell can be seen as a node. The goal is to identify nodes that can reach two specific boundaries (Pacific and Atlantic). This signals the need for depth-first or breadth-first search techniques.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Check each cell individually to see if it can flow to both oceans by performing a search from that cell.
approaches.algorithm: For each cell, perform a DFS/BFS to see if it can reach the Pacific and Atlantic edges.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Use a reverse traversal starting from the Pacific and Atlantic edges to mark reachable cells, leveraging the properties of water flow.
approaches.algorithm: Perform DFS/BFS from both the Pacific and Atlantic edges simultaneously, marking cells that can flow to either ocean.

dry_run: Consider the matrix: [[1,2,2,3,5],[3,2,3,4,4],[2,4,5,3,1],[6,5,4,2,2],[5,1,1,2,4]]. Start from the Pacific edge (top row and left column) and perform DFS. Mark reachable cells. Repeat for the Atlantic edge (bottom row and right column). The intersection of marked cells gives the result.
edge_cases: An empty matrix should return an empty list. A single cell matrix should return that cell if it can flow to both oceans. Duplicates in the matrix should not affect the flow logic. Large matrices should be handled efficiently to avoid timeouts.
common_pitfalls: 1) Failing to account for boundary conditions can lead to incorrect results. 2) Not marking cells as visited can cause infinite loops. 3) Misunderstanding the flow direction can lead to incorrect assumptions about which cells can reach which ocean.
